Day 12: my first PC upgrade — from no-more-cafés punishment to learning HDD, SSD, and RAM
My first PC upgrade: from no-more-cafés punishment to learning HDDs, SSDs, and RAM
Remember in the previous post when I said my dad brought home a second computer? Let's talk about that today. One day something happened…
"Why are you still out at this hour?" my dad asked me.
It was around 7–8 PM. By rights I should've been home by 4 (when school ended), but ever since I'd started hanging out at internet cafés, I'd often slide in around 8.
"I was at school playing ball..." my standard excuse. My dad really opposed me going to cafés — to him, they were a bad-influence place.
"Have you looked at what time it is?" my dad pointed at the wall clock.
"You can't possibly have been playing ball this late!" my dad said, getting irritated.
"Just say you went to the café — stop with the ball talk!" my dad continued.
"…" I was surprised — my dad knew I'd been at the café.
"Surprised? Didn't think I'd know you'd been at cafés, did you?" he continued.
"No more cafés. If you want to use a computer, I'll bring home another one from the company!" my dad finalized.
For me at the time, this was devastating. I'd made friends at the café — we'd game and chat together, the vibe was great. I had complaints, but no choice. I had to refocus on the new computer.
"OS" here means "off-screen" — inner thoughts, like internal monologue.
But this was another retired company computer, and it had a pile of issues — slow boot, frequent crashes, BSoDs — really frustrating.
Luckily I knew a more knowledgeable senior at school (or actually
So I asked the senior at school what to do about the problem computer at home. I remember him saying...
"Do you know how to reinstall the OS?" he asked.
"Pretty much. I saw some info at the café — I can try it." I answered.
"Then go try reinstalling. I have a USB with a Windows installer. Take it with you." He handed me the USB.
"While you're at it, swap the HDD for an SSD, and bump RAM to 8GB to try it." he added.
"Huh?? HDD to SSD? RAM to 8GB? What do these even mean?" my brain thought.
So I just said "OK" to the senior and went home.
How I worked up the courage to ask my dad for the parts is another story.
In "Day 3: Motherboard Maze — The First Time I Opened the Case", we touched on PC internals briefly, including HDD vs SSD. Let's talk about both today.
HDD (Hard Disk Drive) is the traditional hard drive. It uses platters and heads to read/write — features include large capacity, long lifespan, low price, which is why it stayed popular.
An HDD looks roughly like this:
Open the metal cover and you see:
stores data. An HDD typically has multiple platters. They spin at high speed (5400 or 7200 RPM) to reduce access latency.
floats above the platter, reading/writing data via magnetic field changes.
supports and moves the head, sweeping radially to access different tracks.
spins the platters at high speed, lets the head find and access data quickly.
connects motor and platters, syncs platter spin.
controls the head arm movement via current changes, positioning the head precisely.
Although HDDs last long, they absolutely can't be shaken while running — head-platter distance can lose balance, causing read failures or even damage.
HDD read/write performance mainly depends on
. Higher RPM = faster access. Common specs: 5400 RPM and 7200 RPM.
RPM = Revolutions Per Minute. 7200 means 7200 revolutions per minute.
Each HDD also has a transfer interface — SATA, SAS, SCSI, etc. — affecting transfer speed and use environment.
SSD = Solid State Drive. Unlike HDDs, SSDs use NAND Flash (flash memory) for storage — no platters, no heads, no moving parts.
"No moving parts" is hard to visualize, so let's look at an SSD.
You can see the internal structure differs clearly from HDDs — just a single PCB:
every SSD has a controller chip — handles read/write, error correction, garbage collection, etc. SSD lifespan and performance highly depend on controller quality.
where data actually lives. Usually multiple chips. If one fails, the whole SSD may be unusable.
SSDs' biggest advantages: low latency, high speed. Performance measured in
— no moving parts, so no concept of "rotation".
SSD transfer interfaces include SATA, NVMe, etc. — they affect SSD read/write speeds too.
SSD lifespan is usually shorter than HDDs. Measured in TBW (Total Bytes Written) — total write volume the SSD can withstand over its lifetime. Bigger TBW = longer life.
Here's a table comparing HDD and SSD at a glance:
Fast (depends on interface, SATA or NVMe)
Higher mechanical failure risk, but magnetic storage handles writes well
Limited write cycles, but modern SSDs last long enough; more reliable in most cases
Smaller (high capacities are expensive, but 4TB–8TB consumer SSDs exist)
Remember what RAM is analogous to in human terms? It's OK if you forgot — RAM is like short-term memory.
Important concept: lots of people confuse RAM and hard drive — they're completely different. RAM is temporary storage; hard drive is permanent.
RAM's main function: temporarily store data being processed. Once the computer powers off, RAM data is gone. That's why RAM is called

"Random access" means data can be read/written at any time, at any location, without ordering. Like grabbing a book from a shelf — you can pull the one you want directly. "Sequential access" means going step-by-step through items until you find what you want.
items in front of you, pick whichever you need.
has to be in order — first find the right shelf → then the right cabinet → then the book.

RAM comes in different specs — DDR3, DDR4, DDR5. Newer = faster, more performant. Each generation has different golden-finger pin layouts and isn't cross-compatible, so check motherboard support before buying.
In MHz (Megahertz) — frequency at which data can be processed per second. Common specs: 2400, 2666, 2933, 3200 MHz. Higher = faster.

Another measurement unit is MT/s (Mega Transfers per second). Due to marketing, most vendors label in MHz. For DDR4-3200, the actual clock is around 1600 MHz, but because DDR is "Double Data Rate", transfer speed is 3200 MT/s. The "3200 MHz" label typically means 3200 MT/s.
RAM capacity is in GB (Gigabyte). Common specs: 4GB, 8GB, 16GB, 32GB. Higher = more capacity.

Mostly data the CPU is actively processing — open programs, documents being edited, browsing tabs — temporarily stored in RAM. When the CPU needs them, it reads from RAM directly, boosting performance and responsiveness.
Smoother large file edits or design work
Anyone else taken out RAM and rubbed the gold fingers with a pencil eraser?

This actually has a scientific basis — RAM gold fingers oxidize with prolonged use, causing poor contact. Gentle wiping with a pencil eraser removes the oxide layer, restoring good contact. Watch the pressure though — scratching the gold fingers might do more harm than good.
